skill.name: ai-marketing-videos
skill.description: Creates structured prompts and command workflows for AI-generated marketing videos using the inference.sh CLI. It covers product ads, launch videos, explainers, voiceovers, music, platform formats, and A/B creative variants.
skill.summary: Build AI marketing video workflows for ads, product launches, explainers, voiceovers, and social platforms.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution findings, but most are false positives caused by Markdown shell examples for the intended inference.sh workflow. The real security concern is the remote install command that pipes a downloaded script into sh, plus normal network dependence on inference.sh-hosted services and media URLs.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Line 16 instructs users to run curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh. This pattern executes remote code before the user can inspect it, so DNS, TLS, hosting, or script compromise could result in arbitrary local code execution. The nearby install note claims checksum verification, so malicious intent is not confirmed, but the pattern remains risky.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit and executes a remote installer. The surrounding text describes intended installation rather than obvious malware, so the finding is high-confidence risk but not proof of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution Is Core Behavior
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE WITH CONTEXT: The skill is designed around Bash(infsh *) and many infsh app run examples. External command execution is expected for this skill, but it can send prompts, media references, and generated assets to third-party inference services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and repeated infsh commands confirm external command use. The commands appear aligned with the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than clearly malicious.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Service And Media URLs
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE WITH LOW SEVERITY: The skill links to inference.sh, cloud.inference.sh, dist.inference.sh, and a placeholder trending-music URL. These URLs support installation, documentation, images, or media merging, and no credential exfiltration or covert callback behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs are visible and semantically connected to the advertised service. No evidence found that they collect secrets or bypass user intent.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static weak cryptography hits at the cited lines do not show cryptographic implementation or credential handling. No evidence found of weak hashing, encryption, password storage, or signature logic in this skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are skill metadata and a sound design heading, not cryptographic code. No implementation performs cryptography in this skill file.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Command Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE FOR RUBY BACKTICKS: The scanner labeled many Markdown fenced command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is documentation, and most flagged lines are example infsh commands rather than hidden dynamic execution inside skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is Markdown code fencing and example commands, not Ruby backtick operators. Some examples still execute commands if copied by a user, so they are not risk-free.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe Downloaded Script To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start uses curl piped directly to sh for CLI installation. Prefer a manual download, checksum verification, and explicit execution steps before publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact command is present on line 16. This is a well-known risky installation pattern even when the provider is legitimate.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Remote Processing
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill sends generation requests and media URLs to inference.sh applications. Users should avoid submitting confidential campaign plans, unreleased product details, or private customer data without approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The infsh commands clearly call remote app models and pass prompts or media references. The data sensitivity depends on user input, so confidence is high for exposure risk but lower for harmful impact.

content.value_statement: Marketing teams need many video concepts, formats, and variants for paid and organic channels. This skill provides reusable AI video workflows for ads, launches, explainers, voiceovers, music, and platform-specific creative.
actual_capabilities: Provides prompt patterns for product launches, brand stories, feature highlights, testimonials, and before-after videos.
Shows inference.sh CLI commands for Veo, Seedance, Kokoro TTS, AI music, and media merging workflows.
Defines video structures for 30-second ads, 15-second social ads, explainers, and platform-specific formats.
Includes guidance for hooks, visual hierarchy, sound design, and mobile-optimized ad checks.
Demonstrates A/B creative variant generation with multiple hooks and output files.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated account for command examples to run.
Generated video quality depends on the selected model, prompt detail, and available service limits.
The skill does not validate brand, legal, music licensing, or advertising compliance requirements.
The quick start includes a remote install command that should be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Launch Product Ads
use_cases.description: Create short launch videos with product reveals, benefit scenes, voiceover, and a call to action.

use_cases.title: Produce Social Variants
use_cases.description: Generate vertical, square, and feed-ready creative variants for Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, and LinkedIn.

use_cases.title: Build Explainer Workflows
use_cases.description: Plan a scripted explainer with scene prompts, narration, transitions, and final assembly steps.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Product Promo
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one short video concept.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 15-second product promo video prompt for [product]. Include the audience, visual style, product benefit, format, and call to action.

prompt_templates.title: Platform-Specific Ad
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for a channel-ready ad.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a [platform] ad video workflow for [product]. Use [aspect ratio], a hook in the first three seconds, benefit-focused visuals, and text overlay space.

prompt_templates.title: Complete Launch Sequence
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for a multi-scene launch ad.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a 30-second product launch video for [product]. Break it into hook, problem, solution, benefits, proof, voiceover, music, and final call to action.

prompt_templates.title: A/B Creative System
prompt_templates.prompt: Create four A/B video ad variants for [campaign]. Vary the hook, emotional angle, visual pacing, and call to action while keeping brand constraints consistent.

output_examples.input: A fitness app needs a 15-second Instagram Reel ad.
output: A vertical video concept with a two-second problem hook, fast app interface moments, lifestyle shots, and a direct trial call to action.
Recommended scenes include morning routine friction, one-tap workout planning, progress celebration, and a final phone hero shot.

output_examples.input: A SaaS company needs a 60-second explainer video.
output: A structured script outline with hook, problem, product solution, three feature scenes, proof point, and closing call to action.
Visual guidance favors clean motion graphics, readable interface moments, professional narration, and simple transitions.

output_examples.input: A hardware brand wants launch video variants.
output: Four creative angles: premium reveal, problem-solution demo, customer testimonial, and feature-focused close-up sequence.
Each variant keeps product visibility high and leaves space for brand text and offer overlays.

best_practices: Review remote install commands before running them, and prefer verified manual installation when possible.
Provide product, audience, platform, aspect ratio, brand tone, and call-to-action details in each request.
Keep campaign secrets, unreleased assets, and customer data out of prompts unless remote processing is approved.
anti_patterns: Do not use one generic video prompt for every platform and aspect ratio.
Do not submit confidential marketing plans or private customer data without approval.
Do not publish generated ads without legal, brand, claims, and music licensing review.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create prompts and command workflows for AI marketing videos, including ads, launch videos, explainers, voiceovers, and variants.

faq.question: Does it generate video directly inside Claude or Codex?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflows that use the inference.sh CLI and remote models to generate or assemble media.

faq.question: Which platforms are covered?
faq.answer: The examples cover Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, YouTube, LinkedIn, websites, and general product demo formats.

faq.question: Is the quick start installation safe?
faq.answer: The quick start uses a remote script piped to sh. Review the script or use manual verification before running it.

faq.question: Can it make voiceovers and music?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include Kokoro text-to-speech, AI music generation, and media merging workflows.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide product details, target audience, platform, duration, aspect ratio, offer, brand tone, and required call to action.
